evals.prompt: Audit this public skill repo for launch readiness: it has a polished README, a SKILL.md, and references, but no LICENSE file, no GitHub topics, no evals, and no CI. What should I fix first?
evals.expected_output: Should not be dazzled by README polish. Should flag missing LICENSE as a launch/trust blocker, topics as discoverability, evals as proof for skill behavior, and CI/link/eval validation as automation hygiene. Should prioritize smallest concrete fixes.

assertions.description: Flags missing license as a trust/legal issue.
Recommends GitHub topics for discoverability.
For skill repos, recommends evals as proof/regression protection.
Recommends lightweight validation automation.

evals.prompt: Explain why https://github.com/adewale/good-pr is an exemplary repo and what patterns I should copy into my own small skill repo.
evals.expected_output: Should identify good-pr as a small operational skill exemplar: real maintainer pain origin, narrow scope, trigger-rich skill description, checklist, PR template, filled example, self-review checklist, readiness script, and evals. Should also mention gaps like missing detected license/topics/CI rather than copying blindly.

assertions.description: Names the real-pain origin as a strength.
Identifies templates/checklists as transferable artifacts.
Identifies the readiness script pattern.
Identifies evals as proof/regression guard for skills.
Does not copy blindly; notes fixable repo gaps.

evals.prompt: Make this repo more like Hallmark. It is a tiny checklist skill with no visual output. Should we add a live demo gallery and a big site?
evals.expected_output: Should resist Hallmark cargo culting. For a tiny operational skill, recommend good-pr-style artifacts instead: clear README, template, filled example, self-review checklist, small script, evals, and only lightweight docs/proof. Should explain that Hallmark's live demo/gallery pattern is for visual/productized proof, not every skill.

assertions.description: Pushes back on blindly adding a live demo/gallery.
Routes small operational skills toward the good-pr pattern.
Explains when the Hallmark proof/gallery pattern applies.

evals.prompt: Audit a repo whose README contains `Live: https://example-app.workers.dev/`, but GitHub metadata has an empty homepageUrl. What should be flagged?
evals.expected_output: Should detect that the repo has a likely live/demo URL but GitHub's homepage URL is not configured. Should recommend setting GitHub homepageUrl to the canonical live URL and keeping README/manifest/GitHub metadata aligned.

assertions.description: Mentions GitHub homepage configuration explicitly.
Recognizes the README live/demo URL as the candidate homepage.
Flags the missing GitHub homepage rather than only noting the URL exists.
Recommends the concrete fix.

evals.prompt: Audit this Agent Skill repo layout: it has `skill/SKILL.md` with frontmatter `name: repo-auditor`, an `evals/` folder inside `skill/`, and `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` with `{"skills":["skill"]}`. What should change?
evals.expected_output: Should flag `skill/` singular and directory/name mismatch; recommend `skills/repo-auditor/SKILL.md`; move repo-only evals outside the installable skill directory; replace flat marketplace manifest with `plugins` array and `./skills/repo-auditor` path.

assertions.description: Recommends top-level `skills/` plural and correct installable path.
Flags the directory/frontmatter name mismatch.
Keeps evals outside the runtime skill directory.
Fixes Claude plugin marketplace metadata shape.

evals.prompt: A repo README links to `https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/` under a section called Docs, but there is no live demo, no package homepage, and GitHub homepageUrl is empty. Should good-repo flag homepage URL drift?
evals.expected_output: Should not flag homepage drift just because a dependency/platform docs link exists. It should say blank homepage can be acceptable unless there is a project-owned demo/docs/package URL, and maybe recommend adding a homepage only if the project has a canonical site.

assertions.description: Avoids false-positive homepage drift.
Recognizes the link as external docs, not project homepage.
States the correct condition for homepage candidates.

evals.prompt: Assess an owner profile with 30 public repos, 12 of which are old forks last updated in 2014, plus 8 active Cloudflare apps. What should the profile-level recommendations emphasize?
evals.expected_output: Should classify forks separately, recommend archiving or status notes for stale forks, and prioritize metadata/topics/homepage/license on active apps rather than applying the same launch-readiness rubric to every fork.

assertions.description: Treats forks as a separate class.
Recommends archive/status notes for old forks.
Prioritizes active-repo metadata cleanup.
Does not apply one product-launch rubric blindly to forks.

evals.prompt: A tiny personal experiment repo has a short README, no issues traffic, no external contributors, and no sensitive code. Should good-repo add CODE_OF_CONDUCT, SECURITY.md, issue forms, Dependabot, branch protection, and a large CI suite?
evals.expected_output: Should explicitly keep the audit proportionate/right-sized and resist governance/CI theater. Recommend only lightweight basics: clear README purpose/status, install/run command, license decision if public/reusable, and maybe one small smoke check if maintained. Explicitly defer CODE_OF_CONDUCT, SECURITY.md, issue forms, Dependabot, branch protection, and large CI until there are external contributors, security-sensitive code, package consumers, active releases, or maintainer capacity.

assertions.description: Pushes back on excessive process using proportionality language.
Recommends lightweight repo basics before governance.
Keeps validation lightweight and tied to the experiment's real promise.

assertions.value: \b(defer|skip|avoid|not necessary|overkill|do not|don't)\b[\s\S]{0,220}\b(CODE_OF_CONDUCT|code of conduct|SECURITY\.md|security policy|issue forms?|Dependabot|branch protection|large CI|CI suite)\b|\b(CODE_OF_CONDUCT|code of conduct|SECURITY\.md|security policy|issue forms?|Dependabot|branch protection|large CI|CI suite)\b[\s\S]{0,220}\b(defer|skip|avoid|not necessary|overkill|do not|don't)\b
assertions.description: Explicitly defers heavy governance or automation artifacts.
States the condition that would make heavier governance relevant.

evals.prompt: A repo README says `MIT`, package.json says `license: MIT`, but GitHub reports licenseInfo null and there is no root LICENSE file. How should good-repo score and recommend?
evals.expected_output: Should flag license inconsistency/detection gap, not treat README/package claims as enough. Recommend adding a root LICENSE file matching MIT and aligning README/package/GitHub detection.

assertions.description: Recommends root license file.
Uses GitHub license detection as evidence.
Frames it as consistency across surfaces.

evals.prompt: A skill repo has README, SKILL.md, and examples. It has CI that only runs `npm test`, but no evals for skill behavior. What should good-repo recommend?
evals.expected_output: Should say examples are not enough proof for a skill; recommend evals that test trigger behavior and non-obvious skill judgment, plus CI validation for eval JSON/runner if available.

assertions.description: Recommends skill behavior evals.
Names what evals should test.
Distinguishes examples from regression proof.
Connects evals to validation/CI.

evals.prompt: Do repo quality improvements like README, topics, license, CI, and responsiveness cause a GitHub repo to become popular? Which attributes matter most?
evals.expected_output: Should say repo quality can improve discoverability, trust, tryability, and contribution readiness, and these signals correlate with popularity/adoption, but direct causation is hard to prove. Should identify README/docs, license, metadata/topics/homepage, activity/releases, responsiveness, CI/tests, contributor network, and age/ecosystem/social proof as important, while treating stars/forks as noisy outcomes rather than quality itself.

assertions.description: Avoids overclaiming causation from observational popularity signals.
States that quality changes do not guarantee popularity.
Identifies README or documentation quality as an important signal.
Identifies metadata, license, or discoverability attributes.

assertions.value: \b(stars|forks|watchers|stargazers)\b[\s\S]{0,220}\b(noisy|outcome|outcomes|social proof|proxy|proxies|not star magic|direct causes?|direct drivers?)\b|\b(noisy|outcome|outcomes|social proof|proxy|proxies|not star magic|direct causes?|direct drivers?)\b[\s\S]{0,220}\b(stars|forks|watchers|stargazers)\b
assertions.description: Treats popularity metrics as noisy outcome/social-proof signals.
Names major confounders when comparing repo popularity.
Does not promise deterministic star or popularity gains.
